answer:
\boxed{A}

The unidirectional transmission at a synapse occurs because **action potentials (electrical signals) propagate toward the axonal terminals**, triggering the release of neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft. The presynaptic axon contains the necessary machinery (voltage-gated calcium channels, synaptic vesicles, and release proteins) to release neurotransmitters, while the postsynaptic dendrite has receptors to detect them. This directional flow ensures efficient and controlled signal transmission from neuron to neuron.
